attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of fatigue, nausea, and mild right upper quadrant discomfort. She returned from a six-month backpacking trip through rural India three weeks ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is mild right upper quadrant tenderness without hepatomegaly. Investigations reveal elevated liver enzymes with alanine transferase 450 U/L (5-40) and positive hepatitis E IgM antibodies.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely mode of transmission in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute hepatitis E virus infection likely acquired during travel in India, where the virus is endemic. The incubation period of 2-6 weeks fits with acquisition during her travels.

Contaminated water is the primary mode of transmission of the hepatitis E virus in endemic regions such as South Asia. Poor sanitation infrastructure leads to faecal contamination of water supplies, facilitating the spread of the virus.

In Australia, most cases of hepatitis E are acquired overseas, whereas undercooked meat (e.g., pigs, deer, wild boar) is the main transmission route.

Sexual contact is not a significant mode of transmission for hepatitis E virus, unlike hepatitis B virus. Person-to-person transmission is uncommon.

Blood transfusion transmission of hepatitis E is possible but very rare. It is mainly a concern in immunocompromised patients and is not a common route of infection.

Mosquito bite transmission of hepatitis E virus does not occur. This mode of transmission is associated with other viruses such as dengue, yellow fever, and Zika virus.

Hepatitis E typically causes a self-limiting illness in immunocompetent individuals. However, it can cause severe disease in pregnant women and may become chronic in immunocompromised patients.
learningPoints: Hepatitis E virus is primarily transmitted via the faecal-oral route through contaminated water in endemic regions.
In Australia, hepatitis E is mainly transmitted through consumption of undercooked meet products (e.g., pork, deer).
references: Antibiotic. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {31 Oct 2024}. https://www.tg.org.au
Ma Z, de Man RA, Kamar N, Pan Q. Chronic hepatitis E: Advancing research and patient care. J Hepatol. 2022;77(4):1109-1123. doi:10.1016/j.jhep.2022.05.006
